topic: Someone finds their parent's draft letter of resignation from decades ago
raw_output:
[story 1]

The brittle, yellowed paper felt alien in my hands, the ink faded to a ghostly smudge. It was Dad’s handwriting, elegant and precise, yet now it felt like an insult. I unfolded the document slowly, my jaw tightening until my teeth ached. The words—a formal resignation from the firm, dated thirty years prior—were a sharp, unexpected barb. I remember the sting immediately, a hot flush creeping up my neck. Why keep this buried? Why let it just sit there, a relic of a life I never truly understood? I crumpled the paper once, a small, furious gesture, before smoothing it out again, my fingers trembling slightly with restrained irritation. It was a testament to a life I was never meant to inherit, a neat, closed chapter that mocked my present uncertainty.

[story 2]

I found the letter tucked inside a forgotten box of old tax documents. The paper was thin, almost translucent under the weak kitchen light. Reading it, I felt a sudden, intense surge of resentment. My chest constricted, a tight knot forming beneath my ribs. My eyes scanned the formal language, focusing on the terms of departure, the quiet finality of it all. *He* had walked away, signed a document, and moved on, leaving behind a silence that now echoed with my own unanswered questions. I let out a short, humorless sound, a sharp expulsion of air. I didn't throw it away; instead, I placed it deliberately on the counter, right next to the overflowing fruit bowl, a silent declaration that whatever that departure represented, it was entirely irrelevant to me now.

[story 3]

It was buried deep within the attic trunk, smelling faintly of cedar and dust. When I finally pulled it out, the paper crackled like dry autumn leaves. The letter detailed a career move, a decision made in haste, a severance from a certain kind of expectation. A bitter taste coated my tongue. I stared at the signature, a ghost of my father’s authority laid bare. A slow, deliberate shake of my head escaped me. I walked to the window, watching the street below, feeling a profound sense of superiority wash over me. That document was proof of a path not taken, a life lived by someone else’s terms. I folded the paper with unnecessary force, ensuring every crease was sharp and unforgiving, and tucked it back into the trunk, sealing away that memory with a cold, hard judgment.
